items.prompt: Under GDPR, when can synthetic datasets be shared more freely?
choices: Whenever they are labeled synthetic, regardless of identifiability
When they are statistically representative yet unlinkable in practice with acceptable re-identification risk
Only when they contain exact copies of real rows
Never, because synthetic data is always exempt from all law
items.explain: Section 10.7.2 states sharing depends on residual identifiability, not the synthetic label alone.

items.prompt: Example 10.19 illustrates inflated model metrics from synthetic-only tests that fail on live data.
items.explain: Example 10.19 describes strong reported accuracy on synthetic loan-default data that fails once deployed on live portfolios.

items.prompt: Bias amplification in synthetic data means:
choices: Generators always remove all demographic skew
Skewed source data can produce synthetic samples that reinforce the same gaps
Fairness metrics never apply to generated rows
GDPR prohibits all synthetic hiring data
items.explain: Section 10.7.4 and Example 10.22 describe generators emitting skewed samples that look diverse yet reinforce source bias.

items.prompt: Name two mitigation practices for ethical synthetic data use.
items.rubric: Mention transparency, validation against real benchmarks, oversight/audits, fairness constraints, or diverse training sources.
items.sample_answer: Disclose generation methods and validate synthetic data against real benchmarks, with ethics oversight and fairness checks before deployment.

items.prompt: Example 10.23 raises whether GDPR data-subject rights may extend to models trained on synthetic derivatives of personal data.
items.explain: Example 10.23 describes regulators questioning rights and provenance for synthetic derivatives of personal data.
